Y’all need to understand that this movie is a prequel to The Lion King from 2019, not the original story. In the original Lion King, *Scar and Mufasa are brothers by blood* .
The producer of The Lion King said it himself that they are related, not to mention the story was inspired by Hamlet, which is about biological brothers.
Besides that, in the original story, they call each other brothers. If they weren’t blood-related, It would have likely been brought up at some point.

Mufasa: The Lion King review: 'Pointless' and a 'contrived cash-in'.

This prequel to a re-make "never picks up momentum" – "Don't the talented artists involved have anything better to do with their time?"

When the CEO of Disney announced in February that the studio would start relying more on "sequels and franchises", he wasn't joking. Already this year, we've had Inside Out 2 and Moana 2, and now we have Mufasa: The Lion King, a prequel to 2019's photorealistic remake of 1994's much-loved cartoon.

Yes, we're talking about a prequel to a remake. And, yes, it's as pointless as that description makes it sound. This contrived cash-in may be worth sitting through on Disney+ if you're a Lion King superfan, but, like so many prequels, it devotes tremendous amounts of thought and energy to answering questions that nobody was asking in the first place. When did Simba's dad Mufasa meet his wife Sarabi? Where did Rafiki the mandrill get his walking stick? How did Zazu the hornbill become Mufasa's right-hand man (or, I suppose, right-paw bird)? The film is directed by Barry Jenkins, who made the Oscar-winning Moonlight, and the songs are by Lin-Manuel Miranda, the most feted Hollywood and Broadway songwriter of his generation, so a more pressing question might be: don't the supremely talented artists involved have anything better to do with their time?
The big question the film answers, however, is how a lowly lion named Mufasa (Aaron Pierre) found his way to the idyllic Pride Lands with a friend who would become known as Scar (Kelvin Harrison Jr) – and I can guarantee that nobody had been asking that question, because it contradicts everything that was established in The Lion King. If you remember, the whole point of the original film was that Mufasa's son Simba was the latest in a long line of monarchs who had protected the Pride Lands for generations, and that Mufasa's younger brother Scar was annoyed about his place in the pecking order. But presumably somebody at Disney became uncomfortable with that feudal premise, so they've scrapped it in favour of a more complicated and egalitarian origin story. The trouble is that this democratic new version fits the lore so badly that the producers might as well have renamed their film Mufasa: The Lion Prime Minister. It's as bad as Padmé being "elected" as the queen of her planet when she was a 14-year-old girl in Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace.

Anyway, the film opens with the news that Simba (Donald Glover) and his wife Nala (Beyoncé) are about to have another cub, so they disappear into a forest without telling their daughter Kiara (Blue Ivy Carter) where they're going or why. (The motivation behind this bizarrely bad bit of parenting is never explained.) Kiara's babysitters are Timon the meerkat (Billy Eichner) and Pumbaa the warthog (Seth Rogen), and together they sit in a cave while Rafiki (John Kani) tells her about her grandfather Mufasa. After this unnecessarily long and complicated set-up, the main plot gets underway. (Spoiler ahead)

The film is all about lions talking to other lions while being pursued by yet more lions, so it can be visually monotonous – it can also be challenging to keep track of which lion is which

The first one only made a billion bucks because of nostalgia, and because they got the biggest stars on the planet to voice the characters. So they didn’t need to give the animals facial expressions to add real emotion to the film. They didn’t have to give any respect to “Be Prepared”, one of the greatest villain songs of all time. They had no problem with the characters singing “Can You Feel The Love Tonight” during the DAY. They had no problem with making Scar so obviously mentally disturbed that he wouldn’t be able fool anybody into doing anything. 

“Yes, the past can hurt. But you can either learn from it, or run from it.” In the original version, Rafiki told this to Simba. It was one of the most important lessons in the film, and they completely skipped over it because baboons don’t hold sticks in real life. But for some reason Rafiki was allowed to have his stick in the fight scene at the end. 

The hill Mufasa was on wasn’t even close to being steep enough for him to fall off of it, but for some reason he acted like he couldn’t climb it. In the remake, Mufasas death was his fault. There’s no reason that he shouldn’t have been able to climb that hill. And Mufasa should’ve realized that in this version, Scar was clearly evil. Scar didn’t try at any point in the new version to act innocent. If Mufasa realized it, he would’ve never ended up in this situation in the first place. 

This new movie doesn’t have nostalgia to lean on as a crutch. They’re telling a new story. They will have to put in some actual effort. The characters will have to actually emote. The villain will have to be more sophisticated, not just pure evil. If they want this movie to perform as well as the first one, they have to make a whole lot of changes. This trailer looks exactly the same as the first one. So I’m predicting this movie isn’t going to perform well at all.

Lion King/Game of Thrones

*Mufasa: The Lion King* is an upcoming prequel to *The Lion King*, set to release on December 20, 2024. Directed by Barry Jenkins, the film will delve into the backstory of Mufasa, the iconic lion king. The movie will explore his rise to power, his relationship with his brother Taka (who later becomes the villain Scar), and how he became the beloved leader of the Pride Lands.

The story will be told through flashbacks, with Rafiki narrating the legend to Kiara, Simba and Nala’s daughter. This film will blend live-action and photoreal CGI, similar to the 2019 *The Lion King*. The cast includes Aaron Pierre as Mufasa, Kelvin Harrison Jr. as Scar, and Tiffany Boone as Sarabi, among others. Also, music will be provided by Lin-Manuel Miranda, known for his work on *Hamilton*. 

Make sure to mark your calendar for its theatrical release in December 2024!

According to the millennia-old tradition of the divine right of kings, a legitimate ruler who acts in accordance with the will of God through virtue and true justice bestows the gifts of prosperity and peace upon society. Under his righteous government, stability and economic well-being flourish, and social order is assured. However, when the throne is occupied by an usurper, a tyrant, or a ruler without legitimacy, the fabric of civilization disintegrates, giving way to chaos, decline, and the scourges of crises, crime, famine, plagues, and wars. The morality and legitimacy of the ruler are the pillars of stability; their absence plunges society into the abyss of disorder. This tradition imposes a double burden on the monarch: his responsibility is not only toward men but also toward God. He must govern with wisdom and fairness, for divine displeasure can be as relentless as that of the people. The fate of a nation is therefore closely tied to the character of its ruler. A king’s inability to maintain the favor and trust of his subjects makes his reign unsustainable, dragging him into an inexorable cycle of crises and suffering. To preserve his legitimacy, it is imperative that the ruler aligns his actions with the sublime values expected by both the deity and the community. This tradition does not merely justify royal authority but elevates royal power to an ethical dimension, where the nation’s prosperity depends on the virtuous behavior of the ruler. Social harmony thus arises from this intimate relationship between ruler and subjects, in which royal justice and morality are regarded as indispensable foundations for collective well-being.

I am disappointed that this is in the live CGI universe and not the OG and uses the birth name for Scar in the non-canon Six New Adventures, Taka meaning 'waste' or 'trash' which fits better in that universe where he was the rejected second son. Considering how this concept clashes with The Lion Guard, I am going to assume the live CGI universe is an alternate TLK. 
Furthermore, I agree with what others have said about the voices, those voices just don't fit the characters. 

The first live CGI seemed to focus its cast on African voice actors, no doubt for authenticity as was done with Harry Potter with the exception of Timon and Pumbaa, hence James Earl Jones being the only OG asked to return. (R.I.P. I honestly wondered if that dude might live forever). What happened to that? 

Granted as an OG fan since its 1994 release, I never could fully accept a Scar without Jeremey Irons' voice so perhaps I am being a bit biased. 

I do, however, appreciate the concept that Scar was the rightful heir and lost it to a brother who wasn't even blood related to him. It gives him more depth and ties more appropriately into the line "when at last I am given my dues" in Scar's villain song 'Be Prepared'.

Scar's name Taka is Swahili for Trash. It comes from the first back story (A Tale of Two Lions). He was the younger brother and his family hated him. Saw him as useless. Which is what led to him becoming evil. 


Plus Mufasa was a spoiled brat. In the Lion Guard the story was more interesting. His name was Askari (Soldier) and since he would never see the throne, his parents had him trained to be a military leader instead. He even gained the roar of the Elders. The reason he became scar is because he stopped an evil Lion and his Serpent side kick from overthrowing the royal family.

Hi Disney. I can guarantee that putting short content, like trailers and updates for released content akin to the one for Deadpool & Wolverine, behind an adwall to already paying Disney+ subscribers will have way more detrimental effects to your pocketbooks in the long term than your advisors are telling you. They're only looking in their own short term interests and banking on the relatively miniscule returns based solely on this tiny boost to ad revenue. Your loss in brand confidence, subscriber base, and long term losses in revenue at your theme parks will far outweigh any short term benefits from this ad revenue. We have already swallowed the bitter pill of ads in movies and shows that we've already paid for; adding very short term content to this will alienate many more patrons than you think. You may look at it as ad revenue per minute of content but we see it as advertisement time per minute of (already paid for) content, and the numbers are really grim. Unless you're already looking to cut and run, this is a terrible look.

I see many upset that Taka was a prince and Mufasa wasn't, but this is my idea on what this movie might be doing:

1)Mufasa mentioned he's lost, meaning he's not a stray(orphan) and was from a different pride, most likely in my opinion pride rock (but got separated from his pride somehow). Mufasa must have gotten separated at a young age or hit his head, so he has little to no memory of where he came from. If anything, he's always been the rightful prince of pride rock. 

2)Taka/Scar's pride doesn't look like they are even in pride rock, so that makes Taka a prince to a different pride.

So, I think as the two journey as young adults, Mufasa learns of his origin, and travels to pride Rock, where he's given back his title as King of Pride Rock. Meanwhile, Taka, who was a prince of a different Pride, lost his pride to the white Lion and slowly loses everything. So, while Mufasa keeps gaining, Taka is slowly losing, becoming more envious and Jealous of the Lion he saved. Thus, why he feels he deserves the place as king, for if it wasn't for him, Mufasa wouldn't even be alive (Ironic I know). Then the two fight, Mufasa give scar the scar, and thus bringing us to where the other "live action" takes place. 

Now maybe Mufasa also steals the lioness scar had a crush on, Sarabi, which doesn't help. But that's my idea of what may happen. Could be wrong, but only time will tell once the movie comes out. :)

Disney needs to stop doing the sympathetic villain trope wrong. The trope works in franchises like Hunger Games or The Wizard Of Oz because they had very mysterious and expansive stories that allowed for a sympathetic villain story. The trope doesn’t work in franchises like Star Wars or Sleeping Beauty or The Lion King because those franchises originated with a clear Good Vs Evil story and that’s what those franchises had stood for for a long time.

Secondly, who are those cubs in the beginning? They can’t be Simba and Nala if Timon and Pumbaa are there too, so they must be Kion and Kiara, right? If so then this leads into the Lion Guard universe, which makes no sense because -

Thirdly, Scar’s real name is NOT Taka, it’s Askari! He was named after the first Lion Guard leader, according to the Lion Guard canon.

And finally, wasn’t James Earl Jones slated to be in this?

OR, despite the trailer’s appearance that the brothers work together to defeat Kimba, it is actually Mufasa who deals the final blow and where Scar’s father is forced to acknowledge Mufasa is more of a king. And why, do you ask, does Taka change to Scar? Because out of humiliation of Kimba giving Taka his scar, Scar’s dad changes his name in front of everyone before acknowledging Mufasa as ruler. The quote from Scar to Mufasa: "As far as brains go, I've got the lion's share, but when it comes to brute strength, I do believe I'm at the shallow end of the gene pool." We're is mufasa father
In comics book Zazu till Ahadi the father of scar and mufasa
ZUZU ABOUT AHADI

Ahadi was well known for his bravery, having solved numerous crises and faced many dangers. Not much could be said about his usage of power; however, it was suggested that he was responsible and used his powers only to the benefit of his subjects rather than to achieve his own ends. This, in turn, benefits the kingdom as a whole, even if some subjects (namely the hyenas) were left unfulfilled or discontent.

When it comes to the will of his subjects, Ahadi will lend an ear, but did not tolerate being talked over and refuses to take the blame for problems that were not his fault. He caters to the complaints of the Pridelanders as best he could, but did not submit himself to subordinates and instead ensures that all laws were obeyed, evident when he reprimands the hyenas for attempting to kill for sport, an action not permitted in the Pride Lands.

Ahadi's firmness isn't limited to his subjects. Even in family matters, he remains firm. Refusing to submit to his own sons, Ahadi goes so far as to break a promise he made to Taka in order to attend to a more important problem. He apologizes, lets Taka take the apology as he will, and moves on, mostly ignoring any animosity Taka retaliates with. Despite this, Ahadi did care deeply for his children and was greatly angered with Boma's herd when they almost kill his son. Ahadi was, however, reprimanding of his son as well for almost getting his family killed.

Physical Appearance
He had a long black mane and bright green eyes.
A PHYSICAL DESCRIPTION OF AHADI

When it comes to size, Ahadi's exact stature was hard to measure, though he appears to be roughly the same size as his eldest son, Mufasa, if not larger. Like his son, Ahadi sports a sturdy body with broad shoulders, rounded muscles, and an expansive chest with the added bulk of his mane. His face was broad and somewhat short, and his jaw is square and strong.

Though his muzzle, paws, and underbelly were all light in color, Ahadi's pelt is noticeably darker, being a dirty gold as opposed to the paler hues of his pride mates. Brighter in color, however, was Ahadi's mane, colored a lush red with darker fur combing the tips. His eyes mirror those of his eldest son's, being reddish-brown. His eye rims, however, are highly unusual, with the top shade being dark while the lower shade is red.

Ahadi passed many traits down to his sons, though the similarities differ depending on the source. According to the illustrations, Ahadi and Mufasa share body structure, fur and eye coloring, and colored paws. However, the text of A Tale of Two Brothers conflicts with the appearance described above, as the author depicts Ahadi as having a black mane and green eyes, as opposed to a red mane and reddish-brown eyes. This description would make Ahadi's appearance akin to that of his

With the earlier trailers I was ready to hate absolutely everything about this film because they ran over the lore by saying Mufasa didn't have a drop of royal blood in him, despite in the original film he states his father was king and they come from a long line of kings. (In the star scene where he teaches Simba about the kings of the past.) Then if you want to go semi-canon, in the Lion King six adventures series, they talk about the past kings of the Pridelands, and in the Disney Jr. show, The Lion Guard, they even mention the first Lion Guard from generations ago. (Something they established only the second born of the royal family is, and it was a lion we had never heard of, implying this has gone on for many, many generations.)

This is the 3rd interpretation of of backstory of Mufasa and Scar
(Spoilers if you are a Lion King media fan)




•Book
Mufasa is Scars (Taka) biological brother. Mufasa is firstborn of the royal bloodline. The childhood is basically the same as they love each other as brothers but in the book Taka gets his scar from a greedy Buffalo. Mufasa and the rest of the lion cubs basically bully him and call him scar. There is also another interpretation within the book that Taka is seen as weak by his father (or does something very bad he’s not supposed to) and almost dies to his father but instead his mother takes his place. This causes a great gap between the family with eventually Taka hating his brother because he sided with their father.

• Lion Guard (Disney Junior Show)
It’s the same as the book but in this universe things are a little different but even for a kids show as a fan it’s very interesting. In the show, a power called The Roar of the Elders is a power that is basically loud roar that can affect the environment to a degree depending on the user’s skill. The Roar is given to the second born cub in the royal family with the eldest just given the position of king/queen. Each second born is given the Roar to lead the Lion Guard, a group of five members, traditionally lions (in the show the main character leads a group consisting of a hippo, ibis who is replaced by a hawk in the 3rd season, cheetah and a honey badger) who are given the position of the fastest, strongest, keenest, bravest and lastly fiercest belonging to the Lion Guard leader. The Roar is a sacred power that when used for chaos can cause great destruction and even cause the user to lose it, perhaps to never get it again (it’s never confirmed in the show). The groups entire purpose is to protect the pridelands from any trouble within and without (Outlands) to establish justice, peace and harmony.

(**Spoilers ahead about Taka’s backstory in the show!**)





In the show, Taka was respected greatly because he shown a natural ability in the Roar from a young age. One day he goes to the outlands and meets an outcast lion who according to Taka (in a song obviously) he tells him how Taka should be king given he has the Roar and can overpower Mufasa and Taka is persuaded to the least that he follows the lion deeper in the outlands. Its right in a moment that the lion was leading Taka to an ambush by a venomous cobra who strikes Taka at his eye, giving him his scar. The outcast tells scar right after that the venom from the cobra will consume his mind and make him go mad and to emotional impulses to use the Roar for inappropriate reasons, which can lead to great destruction of the pridelands. In the show the Scar actually is symbolically called the “The Scar” because it was a scar located at the eye, which is connected to the brain, can make the user lose control and a possible tactic by the Outlanders to takeover the Pridelands (basically child eco-weapon :p). Anyway, Taka gets furious and uses the Roar at both his adversaries, sending them hundreds of kilometers in the air, erupts a small volcano and theoretically killing them or just sending them away given this is a kids show. 

Scar returns to the pridelands expecting praise, honor and respect from his brother Mufasa for dealing with a potential threat to the Pridelands but in his utter disappointment Mufasa shrugs it off, scuffs his hair, walks away and gives him the nickname Scar. Now, because of the venom his impulses are harder to control so now he goes to meet with his Lion Guard to rally up and kill Mufasa (so he can be King) but they refuse. Scar loses control letting out his final roar that pretty much kills all the 4 members, losing his roar. After that scene in the song it goes to the wilderbeast stampede where Scar kills Mufasa, so he never gets Roar back.

A Theory that I have: Taka and Simba get to the pride lands and now they have to decide who’s gonna become king. I don’t know how ( There’s a lot of theories going around about how Mufasa becomes king instead of Scar ). But in the 2019 version, it’s told that Taka and Simba ended up fighting, which is how Taka got his Scar ( hence the name ). So I imagine, that they ended up brawling this out, but Mufasa ended up winning. Instead of exiling him, he allows ( Taka now Scar ), to stay ( obviously because they were close ). Also in the 2019 version, Scar had history with Surabi. I imagine that they were in the process of becoming mates. But the fight that he and Mufasa had won over Surabi’s affections. But with the fact that Scar originally saved Mufasa, him being the rightful king only to lose it to his “brother”, the fight, losing his gf, and now the physical and literal reminder that he has now lost, builds resentment towards Mufasa. And when Simba is born, it’s solidified that Scar will never be king….which tips him over the edge ( que the lion king )

As a true Lion King lover, I honestly wish this movie was animated the traditional way. 

Traditional drawn animation style would still grant the experience of watching animation, would enhance all emotional expressions of the characters and grant more room for creativity.

The objective of CGI movies - especially of real life animals is somewhat confusing to me. If you want to resemble real life animals - make it realistic. Lions have genitals, and they don't speak or dance. They also don't evoke much emotions like smiling or crying. Lion King might be one of the most emotional movies I have watched, and it just doesn't stick to try to show it from a realistic perspective because you minimize the reason why the movie was so successful in the first place. 

Keen to hear your thoughts - about not only this movie, but the entire transition from traditional animation to CGI and 3D, in particular the remakes.

Almost seems like they’re leaving Taka’s pride when older like a lot of male lions do. Wonder if that’s how they’ll come across the Pridelands. Basically meaning that Taka wasn’t born in the Pridelands and both Mufasa and Taka start their new pride there. Maybe after something happens to the current pride that they were raised in.

Personally, I’m happy it’s a completely new story. I feel like this one is a massive upgrade from the last. The lions are animated with expressions finally and it’s a new story which I’m happy about. 

I feel like there’s so many conflicting headcanons about Scar and Mufasa’s childhood that Disney would’ve pissed people off whichever way they went. There’s the unofficial book “A Tale of Two Brothers”, there’s The Lion Guard, there’s people all over the Internet telling 15 different versions of both of those. Disney probably decided to just make their own story to not push one canon over the other. And I can respect it. I’m just happy to finally just see something new. And the story seems to be going for a “found family” theme which is interesting. And I could just be misremembering, but now that I think of it there’s nothing in my memory that blocks the headcanon of Scar and Mufasa not being biologically related. There’s Mufasa talking about his father telling him about the Kings of the Past, but that could just be a story lion families tell, or maybe Mufasa’s father was the King of another pride. There’s Scar and Mufasa calling each other ‘brother’ but if Mufasa was his adopted brother, then yeah that makes sense. I mean not to mention, they don’t look alike at all lol. 

Either way, I think it’ll be interesting. I’m one of the people who I remember was pressuring Disney that I wanted the 2019 remake to be so similar to the original, I wanted all the scenes to be included, and everything. The film was a mess. Maybe with a new direction they can make an entertaining story.

My thoughts on this movie are really mixed, especially as a long time fan of TLKl,. I dislike the idea that they changed the lore of them being biologically related and Mufasa being the original heir to the throne, but it looks from the trailer that Mufasa probably didn't become king of the pride scar was originally from going based off the information from this trailer. 
I'm assuming they're going the route of "pride gets attacked by white lions and Mufasa and taka have to leave their home to survive" followed by them finding pride rock and discovering a pride there and finding out that this was Mufasa's original home. This though doesn't make sense of why taka would want to be king so badly of this new pride he's not associated with and kill his brother over it. The only way it would make sense is if they made a promise to each other to lead together/do everything together (which would make the most sense given the worldbuilding we're given in this trailer) and Mufasa supposedly goes back on his promise (it's kind of hinted when the white lion says there can only be one king, but why would Mufasa go back on a promise if he did indeed make one just because of what some random lion that attacked their home says?) 
Now going to the actual animation/voice acting itself. The animation looks uh...interesting. i know it's CGI but come on disney, some of the scenes look just so bad. I hope this is not the final product and just a WIP/trailer teaser. The environment is fine visually but that doesn't really matter imo if the characters are animated poorly and don't seem to fit in with their environment at certain points. I feel we're past the point in technology where multi billion dollar companies can make a movie without weird clipping/AI-looking sequences (mainly the part where Obasi is talking) or the lip syncing is really poorly done (which I hope is just bad editing on the trailer's part and not what it actually looks like in the movie). It's really going to bother me otherwise if it's just... like that.
It also really bothers me that the characters are in tense situations but the voices just don't match the characters' emotions at all, or what their emotions SHOULD be. Specifically during the scene where Taka and Mufasa first meet - they're talking at room volume and in a normal tone as if they're standing in front of each other, but they're a notable distance apart from each other while Mufasa is visibly struggling to stay atop the log. This doesn't make any sense - there's no danger in his voice in that scene when a lost child in reality WOULD be scared (it's a movie about talking lions, but still there has to be some standard, and there hasn't been any worldbuilding really that would imply that a cub wouldn't be scared/struggling to breathe/avoid taking in water while avoiding drowning).
The singing, like the last live action TLK movie,  while the voices themselves aren't bad or anything by any means, it still feels...idk out of place or that the characters don't have the right amount of energy for what they're singing about or just downright cheesy lyrics. Unless there's a REALLY good song in there, I feel like it's just going to be mid. The last movie had this same issue.
Speaking of the last movie though, I will say that Jon Favreau is probably not the director because there's actual emotion in the characters LOL. There was some emotion in the last movie, but it was sorely lacking (and I didn't dislike the movie, let me just say). I'm very glad they went a more expressive route instead of making it more lifeless for the sake of "realism". Nonetheless, I will have to wait for the movie to come out in order to make my full opinion on the movie and am lowkey kinda excited to see it.

I just can't get past how bad they've messed up the backstory. Scar and Mufasa not only being not related by blood, but to make Mufasa an outsider who became king completely breaks the lore of the long line of kings he's supposed to have come from. Even if they do the whole "actually he's the lost prince who really was supposed to be king all along" thing, that still changes a lot. Mufasa has always exhuded an air of royalty from birth, raised to be king by his father and mother, while his younger brother was always jealous, and grew up resentful of his brother. Plus, they seem to be doing the "Pride Rock discovery" route, so it's not this longstanding home of these lion kings for generations, but one that Mufasa just discovered and made his home. The whole thing just screams modern "original" storytelling and rewriting for the sake of the writer putting their own spin on things. Mufasa came from nowhere to show that anyone could rise up to become king, because we want him to be a good guy, but if he was born to be king and always raised like that, well then he's one of the privileged rich people, who are bad we dont like them. Scar was the first Disney villain who soared to the top of the list for targeting his own family. Before him, all Disney villains were not related to the hero at all or were their family through marriage/adoption. Scar killed someone he was always connected to, and it was simply because he was jealous that he wasn't chosen to be king. Even when it made sense why he didn't. Made it very impactful and horrifying to have a Disney villain who didn't even love the family that had always loved him.

The white lion is too similar to “Kimba The White Lion” a 1950-60s Japanese cartoon about a white lion named Kimba (Leo in Japan) who after his mother's death: "stars in the sky form the face of his mother, who encourages him.”
In the series, Burazza is a "wise mandrill who is Leo's mentor."
Coco is  a "green parrot who spent some time living with humans and believes that he should be put in charge of mentoring Leo."
Bubu is a "one-eyed lion who wants Leo and his family dead so that he may take the role of Jungle Emperor for himself." 
Dick is a "lanky hyena, almost always seen with his confederate Bo, who works with Bubu and Sylvester in their fiendish plans."
(According to Wikipedia). I see some similarities 🤔
